checks.a_analysis: The output JSON contains text that is entirely derived from the source document. The headings and table cell contents accurately reflect the information presented on both pages of the source PDF. There are no hallucinated introductions, conclusions, or fabricated sections.
The source document contains no contact information (phone numbers, emails, addresses, or URLs), and neither does the output. No information was fabricated.

The output faithfully includes both sections corresponding to the two pages of the source document. Page 1 content (Framework Layers and partial Other Important Layers) and Page 2 content (continuation of Other Important Layers) are all accounted for in the JSON structure.

The tables in the output contain the same number of data rows as the source document. Page 1 contains 10 rows for 'Framework Layers' (including headers) and 19 rows for the 'Other Important Layers' section. Page 2 contains 11 rows representing the continuation of the list. No rows are missing.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: There are no significant text omissions. The descriptions for each layer, including technical terms like 'SSURGO' and 'TINs?', and specific details like '2000 US Census Bureau data' or '1% annual chance', are all preserved exactly as they appear in the source.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The wording in the output matches the source document exactly. The split of the 'Wetlands' description across the page break is handled correctly between the end of the first table and the beginning of the second table, ensuring no information loss.

checks.a_analysis: The document title 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' appears at the top of both Page 1 and Page 2 in the output. This accurately reflects the source document where the title is repeated as a page header. Per the instructions, intentional repetition in the source (like headers on every page) is not a defect. No other instances of duplicated content were found.

The output assigns H1 levels to the document title on both pages. While there are no H2 or H3 levels, the hierarchy is flat rather than broken (no skipping levels like H1 to H3). This is an acceptable normalization for multi-page documents where titles are repeated.

On Page 2, the table structure is corrupted. The first row, which contains the continuation of the 'Wetlands' description text from the bottom of Page 1, is incorrectly identified as a header row ('is_header_row': true). Data that is clearly part of a previous row's body description is being misidentified as table metadata. Additionally, on Page 1, the row 'Other Important Layers | Description' acts as a structural sub-header in the source but is classified as a standard data row in the output, though the Page 2 issue is more severe as it mislabels data as a header.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 2, first row of the table: 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'
checks.b_evidence_output: "is_header_row": true for the row containing "Wildlife Service, National Wetlands\nInventory"

checks.a_analysis: The reading order follows the logical top-to-bottom and page-by-page flow of the source document, moving from the page title into the table content.

The source document relies on tables to present its data; no formal bulleted or numbered lists are present. The conversion correctly handles the tabular structure (with the exception of the header defect noted in S3).
No links or URLs are present in the source PDF, and none have been erroneously added to the output JSON.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the converted document matches the source PDF. The document follows a top-down flow starting with a title followed by a data table. Although the single conceptual table from the PDF is split into two tables in the HTML (reflecting the original page break), the sequence and grouping of information remain consistent. A user can find the information in the same relative locations.

The tables in the HTML output are well-rendered with distinct borders, clear columns, and readable text. The headers are visually distinct. While the 'Wetlands' row is split between two tables, this accurately reflects the pagination of the source PDF where the description continues from the bottom of page 1 to the top of page 2. The alignment of columns within the tables is correct, and the content is legible and logically structured.

The source PDF contains only text and tables; there are no meaningful images, logos, or charts. The HTML output correctly reflects this by also containing only text and table elements. No content has been lost due to missing or broken image assets.

The main document heading 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' is visually duplicated in the body of the converted HTML. In the source PDF, this heading appears only once at the top of the first page. In the HTML output, it appears at the top of the document and is then repeated immediately before the second table section (which contains content from page 2). This is a duplication of body content that is not present in the source material.
checks.b_evidence_source: NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve (appears once on Page 1)
checks.b_evidence_output: NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve (appears twice in the body flow, once at the start and once before the second table)
